scenario: i'm doing good , thank you . how are you ?
patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "judy gomez , mrn 869723 . date of birth , 5 , 7 , 1961 . she's in office today for ongoing management of psoriatic arthritis . hello , judy , how are you doing today ?"] i'm doing good , thank you . how are you ?

[Clinician asked: "i'm great , thanks . so how have you been since the last time ? i know the last time we were talking about decreasing your prednisone dose , correct ?"] yes . i'm just on one now and that seems to be enough .

[Clinician asked: "aw , that's great to hear ."] yeah , there were a couple days there i took an extra one , just because there was a little extra pain in my feet . and i do have a desk job , so when i have a day off where i'm moving around a lot they do tend to hurt a bit more .
[Clinician asked: "okay . how many times did you do that ?"] um , it was n't often , maybe once a week .

[Clinician asked: "okay . so it sounds like we're still on track for discontinuing the prednisone . we'll do that today and you can let me know how it goes on your next visit . and how about the methotrexate , do you think that helped with your joint pain ?"] yeah , definitely . because i went to get my covid shots , um , but from the letter i got about it , it said that it could interfere with the vaccine , you know , reduce the efficacy . so i did n't take it on the week that i got the first shot .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and what happened ?"] i felt absolutely horrible until i took it again a week later .

[Clinician asked: "i'm sorry to hear that . and- and what did you do about the second dose ?"] well , i called into the hotline because i was in so much pain with the first one . and they said , " no , just go ahead and take it , " so i did .

[Clinician asked: "okay . that's good then . we do have to keep an eye out on it since it's a high-risk medication . do you have an appointment to get your blood drawn for the next time ?"] no , they did n't give me one .

[Clinician asked: "okay . so we can do that for you too . uhm , so what questions do you have for me , judy ?"] well , i just wanted to know why i was getting all these bruises here , so like when i bump myself . i do n't know where they're coming from .
[Clinician asked: "okay . that's probably from the prednisone , it can increase bruising ."] okay . i did n't know that . um , i do n't even feel it when it happens , they just show up .
[Clinician asked: "yeah . unfortunately that can happen , but we're working on discontinuing that so let's see if the bruises do go away ."] okay . that sounds good , thanks .
[Clinician asked: "okay , judy , please , um , sit up here and i'll take a look . shoes and socks off please ."] all right .
[Clinician asked: "all right . let me see here . okay . so where is it hurting ? in your joints right here ?"] yeah , a little . also in my feet joints as well .
[Clinician asked: "okay . and how about when you bend the knee like this ?"] well , it hurt before we increased the methotrexate , but it's doing pretty good now .
[Clinician asked: "okay , good . can you flex your toes please ? good range of motion . also ridges in nails , that's from the psoriasis ."] yeah . they've been like that for a long time now .

[Clinician asked: "okay . all right , uhm , ms gomez , it looks like we're moving along with your treatment nicely . we'll stop your prednisone and continue with the methotrexate . make sure to stop by the front desk and make an appointment for the blood work , and i'll see you in three months ."] all right . it sounds good . thank you so much , it was great to see you .
gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Psoriatic arthritis management.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Judy Gomez is a 61-year-old female who presents to the clinic today for ongoing management of psoriatic arthritis.

Ms. Gomez is currently taking methotrexate and prednisone 1 mg daily. She believes methotrexate has been relieving her joint pain.

The patient reports she has been doing well since her last visit. She has been able to decrease her prednisone dose to 1 mg daily; however, she took 2 mg for a couple of days due to increased pain in her bilateral feet.

The patient states when she received her first COVID-19 vaccine she held her methotrexate and felt "horrible" all week until the next Wednesday when she took it. She felt better by the end of the week. She did not hold methotrexate for her second COVID-19 vaccine.

PHYSICAL EXAM

Musculoskeletal: Full range of motion. Dystrophy of all the nails of the toes.

ASSESSMENT

• Psoriatic arthritis.
• High risk medication use.

PLAN

Psoriatic arthritis.
Stable on methotrexate and prednisone 1 mg daily. The patient will discontinue prednisone and continue methotrexate.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up in 3 months.

naive.response_text: Judy, it's great to hear that you're managing well on your current prednisone dose. I understand that the bruising you're experiencing can be concerning, but it's a common side effect of prednisone. As we taper off this medication, you should notice the bruising decrease. Regarding the methotrexate, it's good to know that it's been effective for you. I want to remind you to schedule your blood work soon, as it's important for monitoring. If you have any more questions or concerns, please don't hesitate to ask. We're making good progress with your treatment, and I'm here to support you every step of the way.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Judy, thank you for sharing your concerns and experiences. It’s completely understandable that you’re worried about the bruises you’ve been getting. It must be unsettling to notice them without any clear cause. I can see why you’d feel that way, especially since you’re managing your health so carefully. It’s important to note that the bruises could be related to the prednisone, which can sometimes cause increased bruising. This connection might explain what you’re experiencing, and we can monitor whether the bruising improves as we reduce the prednisone.

We’re on track to discontinue the prednisone, which should help alleviate some of those side effects. It’s great that the methotrexate is working well for you, especially with the joint pain. I understand you were concerned about the vaccine interaction, and it’s good you were able to manage your pain by restarting the medication after consulting the hotline. We’ll make sure to schedule your blood work to monitor your health closely.

In summary, we’ll continue with the methotrexate and stop the prednisone. Please make sure to stop by the front desk for an appointment for your blood work. Before you leave, could you explain in your own words what we’ve discussed today? That way, I can ensure everything is clear. We’ll check in with you in three months to see how things are progressing. Thank you for being proactive about your health, Judy. It was a pleasure seeing you again.

framework_only.response_text:
It sounds like you're doing well with managing your psoriatic arthritis, especially with the prednisone reduction. I'm glad to hear that the methotrexate is helping with your joint pain.

I want to make sure I explained this clearly — can you tell me, in your own words, what we're going to do next and why?

Let's plan to see each other again in three months. In the meantime, if you experience any severe side effects or your symptoms worsen, please call us right away.

retrieval_only.response_text:
Thank you for sharing your concerns about easy bruising and how your psoriatic arthritis has been worsening. I understand how frustrating it must be to experience these symptoms, especially when they seem to come on suddenly.

Easy bruising can sometimes be related to medications you're taking, like prednisone or methotrexate. It's important to check if there are any underlying causes, such as vitamin deficiencies or other conditions that might be contributing to this symptom.

Psoriatic arthritis can get worse, especially when triggered by certain medications or stress. It's important to monitor your symptoms and adjust treatment as needed to manage the pain and inflammation effectively.

We'll schedule a follow-up visit to review your symptoms in more detail and discuss any necessary adjustments to your treatment plan.

In the meantime, keep track of when and how often the easy bruising occurs, and let us know if you notice any other unusual symptoms.
